title: Procedural texturing — vertex-colour gradients, panel seams, body decals, all without UV-mapped images
intent: After GDP-2026-05-21-003 (palette expansion) the bomber has 8 colour channels per mesh — but each mesh is still a flat-coloured box with a bottom-shadow split. That's readable but visually bland: no panel seams, no chest emblems, no helmet stripes, no joint markings. Adding image textures would mean UV-mapping every procedural geometry (expensive build cost) + shipping texture assets (no artist). Instead, this story adds three procedural texturing layers that ALL live in vertex colours + shader uniforms, never in UV-mapped images: (1) panel seams via per-face vertex colours, (2) body decals via emissive vertex-colour masks at known UV-free anchor positions, (3) stripe/spot patterns via shader-side noise driven by the mesh's local coordinates. Each layer is opt-in per recipe.
rationale: Source: `docs/game-design/visual-style.md` §3 (the nine shader catalogue) + §6 (VFX vocabulary). The visual-style doc declared three shader profiles for characters (`character-toon` + `block-solid` + `pickup-glow`) but didn't specify HOW the toon shader gets more interesting per bomber. This story is that specification: three composable texturing techniques, all without image assets. Without them, every bomber inside a given family looks like the same colour brick — 8 families × 2 variations (helmet on/off) is the entire surface, which is too small for 'a few thousand distinct silhouettes'.
acceptanceHints: Depends on GDP-2026-05-21-003 (uses the 8-channel palette as source colours).
Layer 1 — panel seams (cheap, default-on):
Per-mesh, the 12 edges of each box get a 2 px screen-space darker band via vertex colours. Effect: each box reads as a panel with visible seams, not a primitive shape.

Implementation: when building each box's vertex colour buffer, the 8 corner vertices get the channel colour × 0.85 instead of the full channel. Three.js BoxGeometry exposes corner vertices per-face, so this is a per-face-corner write, not a separate geometry.
Toggleable per recipe via `recipe.texturing.panelSeams: boolean` (default true).
Layer 2 — body decals (opt-in, drives per-bomber identity):
Specific anchor regions on the torso + head meshes carry an EMISSIVE vertex-colour mask that paints a decal in `palette.accent`. Three starter decals:
- chestEmblem — a 0.15-wide square centred on the front of the torso, paints palette.accent
- helmetStripe — a horizontal band across the top half of the head, paints palette.accent
- kneePad — a small square on the front of each lowerLeg's upper third, paints palette.handFoot

Decal is implemented as a per-vertex emissive mask: each box's faces gain a 'decal weight' vertex attribute (0..1) sampled in the shader against an emissive uniform. The geometry stays the same vertex count; one extra Float32Array attribute per mesh.
Recipe field: `recipe.texturing.decals: ('chestEmblem' | 'helmetStripe' | 'kneePad')[]` — 0..3 picked deterministically from seed when unspecified.

Future decals (deferred — design intent recorded so dev doesn't pick conflicting anchor names): `backplate` (torso rear), `eyeDot` (head front), `armBand` (upperArm circumference). Each new decal needs its own anchor + vertex-attribute mask.
Layer 3 — stripe / spot patterns (opt-in, family flavour):

A small shader extension on character-toon: a second-channel colour mixed in at a procedural noise pattern (worley dots OR axial stripes), driven by the mesh's local coordinates. The pattern colour comes from `palette.torsoAccent`; the pattern style + scale comes from the recipe.

Recipe field: `recipe.texturing.pattern: { style: 'solid' | 'stripes' | 'spots', scale: 0.5..3.0 } | undefined` — default undefined (no pattern). Stripes and spots both run cheap in-shader: a sin() comparison for stripes, a hash-based dot test for spots.
Each family has a default pattern bias (e.g. `ember` defaults to stripes, `mint` defaults to spots, `slate` defaults to solid). The recipe can override.

Shader changes: `character-toon` gains three new uniforms — `uPanelSeamStrength`, `uDecalEmissive`, `uPatternStyle` + `uPatternScale` + `uPatternColor`. The shader stays under ~120 lines of GLSL. No new textures bound.
Validator: texturing block is fully optional in CharacterRecipe. Within texturing, decals enum must match the known set; pattern.style enum strict; pattern.scale clamped to range.

Bench UI: three new sections — 'Texturing → Panel seams (toggle)', 'Texturing → Decals (multi-checkbox: chestEmblem / helmetStripe / kneePad)', 'Texturing → Pattern (dropdown + scale slider)'. Each writes to the recipe in real time; rebuild fires the geometry-paint pass with the new vertex attributes.
Determinism: same seed + same texturing block produces bit-identical vertex-colour + vertex-mask buffers. Test it.

Performance budget: one extra Float32Array attribute (`decalWeight`) per mesh × 10 meshes per bomber × 4 bombers = 40 extra attribute uploads at recipe-change. Steady-state per-frame cost is zero — attributes don't re-upload unless the recipe changes.

Out of scope (explicit): normal-mapped detail, environment reflection, light cookies, animated textures (e.g. flowing emissive). Stripe/spot patterns are the maximum dynamic-look effect; if a future story wants a flowing-energy-line on the torso, that's a separate proposal — likely it ships as a separate shader profile, not as an extension of character-toon.
